meta.description: Invariant nodes for the sliding window family. An invariant is a structural property that must hold at every step of computation. Invariants are the correctness guarantees — not algorithm names.

field_notes.statement: The precise logical condition that must hold.
field_notes.maintained_by: The operation that preserves this invariant.
field_notes.violated_by: The condition or operation that destroys it.
field_notes.proof_sketch: One-line informal justification of why the invariant holds.
field_notes.breaks_if: Conditions under which this invariant is structurally impossible to maintain.
field_notes.preserves: State archetype IDs whose correctness depends on this invariant.
field_notes.manifests_meta_pattern: The higher-level cognitive primitive this invariant is an instance of.

nodes.label: Window validity invariant
nodes.statement: At every step, the window [l, r] satisfies the constraint. After each expansion of r, l is advanced until the constraint holds.
nodes.maintained_by: Shrink l (advance left pointer) until the constraint is satisfied after each r expansion
nodes.violated_by: Expanding r without checking the constraint; or applying a non-monotone constraint where shrinking l does not restore validity
nodes.proof_sketch: Induction: if [l,r] is valid and we expand to [l,r+1], any violation is fixed by advancing l since removing a left element can only reduce the constraint load (for monotone constraints)
breaks_if: Constraint is non-monotone in window size (adding an element can make the constraint easier to satisfy)
Elements can be negative in a sum constraint

nodes.label: Monotone feasibility invariant
nodes.statement: Expanding the window (increasing r while l is fixed) can only make the constraint harder to satisfy or leave it unchanged. Shrinking the window (advancing l) can only make it easier.
nodes.maintained_by: Problem structure — constraint must be monotone increasing in window size
nodes.violated_by: Negative elements in a sum constraint; non-additive constraints; constraints that depend on absolute positions
nodes.proof_sketch: For sum constraints with all positive elements: sum([l..r+1]) = sum([l..r]) + arr[r+1] >= sum([l..r]) since arr[r+1] > 0. Monotonicity follows.
breaks_if: Array contains negative integers and constraint is on sum
Constraint is 'exactly k distinct' rather than 'at most k distinct'

nodes.label: Monotone queue invariant
nodes.statement: The deque D contains indices in strictly decreasing order of their array values: arr[D[0]] > arr[D[1]] > ... > arr[D[k-1]]. The front index D[0] is always the index of the maximum element in the current window [l, r].
nodes.maintained_by: Before pushing index r: pop all indices from the back while arr[D.back()] <= arr[r]. After advancing l: pop from the front while D.front() < l.
nodes.violated_by: Pushing without back-popping dominated elements; forgetting to evict out-of-range indices from the front
nodes.proof_sketch: Back-popping ensures any index with value <= current can never be a future maximum (current dominates it for any future window). Front-popping ensures the maximum candidate is always within the window bounds.
breaks_if: Deque front eviction (out-of-window check) is omitted
Deque back-pop condition uses strict inequality when equal elements need special handling
